cataloging_subject.label: Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten. X. Zu den ‘Signa ante Judicium’
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Hermann Varnhagen’s complete 1880 Anglia article as encoded in CME00100: two German prefatory paragraphs; a 398-line Cambridge Fifteen Signs work; a continuous Cotton sequence that modern DIMEV divides into a 56-line Invocation to the Creator and a 301-line Fifteen Signs work; German textual notes containing eight quoted Middle English comparison lines; the closing signature; and a German addendum. It is an edited selection containing three abstract medieval works, not one medieval poem, one manuscript witness, one digital encoding, or the complete contents of either manuscript.

catalog_summary.title: Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten. X. Zu den ‘Signa ante Judicium’
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English authors or compilers
catalog_summary.date: Medieval components attested from 1272-1282 to the mid-fifteenth century; edited selection first published 1880; composition dates unknown
catalog_summary.region: England; Cambridge witness language localized to Leicestershire; Cotton dialect unresolved; Digby collateral target language localized to north-west Gloucestershire

titles.value: Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten. X. Zu den ‘Signa ante Judicium’
On Middle English Poems. X. On the ‘Signs before Judgment’

assertion.notes: A supplied English rendering for discovery; the German title remains preferred.

titles.value: Here foloweþ þe .XV. tokenys before the day of dome
notes: The XML and Varnhagen print the rubric; DIMEV records it at current f. 50va.

titles.value: The grace of the Holy Ghost
The grace of the holy goste, That ys ay stedfaste
That we may to the blysse wende, That lastyth ay wythowten ende
Almyȝty god, þat all hath wroght, Heven and helle and erþe of noght
That we synnefull were fallen fro, Thorow Adam synne and Eve also
notes: This is the abstract-work boundary inside the XML’s first 74-line Cotton LG.

titles.value: The Fifteen Signs before the Day of Judgment, prefaced by a short invocation of nine couplets
Kyng of heven, blessed þou be. Lorde of grace and of pyte
When we have done here our ende, To þy ryche blysse þat we mowe wende. Amen for charite!

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English authors or compilers
notes: A cataloging collective for three separately indexed medieval works; it does not assert common authorship.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English authors or compilers

assertion.notes: No named medieval author is supplied for any of the three DIMEV records. The collective display does not merge them into one authored work.

scope.description: selection and arrangement of the complete 1880 article

notes: This covers Varnhagen’s direct Cambridge and Cotton transcriptions. Stengel is the direct transcriber of the separately compared Digby text.
This dates Varnhagen’s edited selection, not composition of the medieval works.

date_statements.display: Mid-fifteenth century (the sole surviving DIMEV 5309 witness)

assertion.notes: DIMEV 5309 has one witness. Its date is a terminus ante quem, not a composition date.

date_statements.display: Cotton copies: second half of the fifteenth century (1450-1499)

assertion.notes: This dates the direct source witness represented in CME00100, not necessarily the earliest surviving witness of either abstract work.

date_statements.display: DIMEV 3000 is also attested in Digby 86, dated 1272-1282

assertion.notes: Digby is a collateral witness of the same DIMEV record and the source of Stengel’s comparison text; it is not the direct source of Varnhagen’s Cotton transcription and does not by itself date composition.
Witness dates and edition dates are not silently converted into composition dates.

places.label: Leicestershire, England
notes: LALME/eLALME LP 531, Grid 445 305, for the Ff.2.38 target hand.
Germany is the article’s publication context and Cambridge, London, and Oxford are holding locations; none is automatically a medieval composition place.
This is a target-hand linguistic localization, not a proven composition site or codex-production place.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The eight quoted comparison lines are Middle English editorial evidence, not additional base-work lines.

form_statements.description: The complete article combines German prose framing and notes with 755 direct Middle English verse lines and eight additional quoted verse lines.
All three indexed medieval works use rhyming couplets; the XML’s line-group boundaries do not always coincide with abstract-work boundaries.
German note prose encloses six quoted Cursor Mundi lines and two quoted Hending lines.

genre_statements.label: Prayer
notes: The Cambridge work begins and ends with prayer; DIMEV 436 is an invocation; DIMEV 3000 has a nine-couplet invocation.

subject_statements.label: The Fifteen Signs before the Day of Judgment
Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment
Creation, cosmology, and the natural world

summaries.value: Hermann Varnhagen’s complete 1880 scholarly article edits two Middle English manuscript treatments of the Fifteen Signs before Judgment and frames them with German introduction, notes, and an addendum. The Cambridge section is one 398-line couplet work, DIMEV 5309. The Cotton section joins a 56-line creation prayer, DIMEV 436, to a distinct 301-line Fifteen Signs work, DIMEV 3000, whose first eighteen lines are an invocation. Varnhagen compares the Cotton signs with Stengel’s printed Digby 86 fragment but leaves their priority unresolved. Two notes additionally quote six lines from Cursor Mundi and two from Hending as comparative evidence.

A four-line invocation to the Holy Ghost and Jesus leads directly into a 394-line account of the fifteen signs preceding Doomsday, closing with a prayer for bliss. DIMEV treats all 398 lines as one work and records Ff.2.38 as its sole witness.

The Cotton section first praises God’s creation and redemption in 56 lines, then begins a separate 301-line Fifteen Signs work with a nine-couplet invocation, narrates the signs, and closes with prayer and ‘Amen for charite.’ A small manuscript paraph marks the second work, but Varnhagen and the XML serialize both inside one 74-line LG followed by a 283-line LG.

The three medieval works are related by subject and editorial juxtaposition but retain separate DIMEV identities. Physical manuscript witnesses, Varnhagen’s and Stengel’s editions, U-M’s encoding, the repository XML, modern catalogs, and digital facsimiles are lineage layers rather than alternate names for the cataloged article.

Eight XML L elements belong to two quotations embedded in Varnhagen’s German footnotes: six Cursor Mundi lines after Cotton base line 144 and two Hending lines after base line 190. They count toward the representation’s 763 total L elements but not the 755 base lines of the three medieval edited works.

extent.description: The complete XML has three prose P elements, six LG elements, and 763 L elements. The three base medieval works total 755 direct lines: Cambridge DIMEV 5309 has 398 lines; Cotton DIMEV 436 has 56; and Cotton DIMEV 3000 has 301. Two LGs nested in editorial notes add eight quoted lines, producing the representation total of 763. Nineteen PB elements cover printed pp. 533-551.

content_parts.label: German editorial preface
content_parts.summary: Two German prose paragraphs survey prior Fifteen Signs scholarship, identify the Cambridge and Cotton witnesses, cite Stengel’s Digby fragment, and leave Cotton-versus-Digby priority unresolved.
notes: The preface discusses broader versions not transcribed in CME00100; those mentions are not treated as contained works.

content_parts.label: Cambridge Fifteen Signs: DIMEV 5309
content_parts.summary: A 398-line Middle English couplet work beginning with a four-line invocation and narrating the fifteen signs before Doomsday.
notes: The four-line first LG is part of DIMEV 5309, not a separately indexed abstract work.

content_parts.label: Cotton Invocation to the Creator: DIMEV 436
content_parts.summary: A 56-line prayer in rhyming couplets praising God’s creation and redemption; the manuscript marginal title reads Quindecim signa and the text leads continuously into DIMEV 3000.
notes: DIMEV separates this work because it also circulates independently and in other witnesses; the XML’s 74-line LG does not mark the boundary after line 56.

content_parts.label: Cotton Fifteen Signs with invocation: DIMEV 3000
content_parts.summary: A 301-line Middle English couplet work: an eighteen-line invocation followed by 283 lines narrating the Fifteen Signs and concluding in prayer.
notes: DIMEV reports a small paraph at the opening, Arabic day numbers, and a large yellow-washed final ‘Amen for charite’ at f. 91rb.
The Digby 86 text is a collateral witness and Stengel’s source, not the direct source of this Cotton transcription.

content_parts.label: Nine-couplet invocation
content_parts.summary: Eighteen lines invoke the king of heaven, ask protection from sin and fiends, and request grace before the signs narrative.

content_parts.label: Fifteen Signs narrative and closing prayer
content_parts.summary: The remaining 283 lines enumerate the signs before the Last Judgment and close with a plea for entry into bliss.

content_parts.label: German notes with Middle English comparison quotations
content_parts.summary: Two German notes quote six Cursor Mundi lines and two Hending lines as comparative evidence.

locators.value: two NOTE elements containing LG; 6 + 2 nested L

notes: These eight lines are not parts of DIMEV 5309, 436, or 3000; their exact quoted edition sources remain unresolved.

content_parts.label: Nachtrag zu s. 533
content_parts.summary: A short German addendum on printed p. 551 follows Varnhagen’s Greifswald signature.

related_works.title: The grace of the Holy Ghost / Þe xv tokenys before the day of dome
notes: The article contains the sole recorded witness version, 398 lines from CUL Ff.2.38.

related_works.title: Almighty God that all hath wrought / Invocation to the Creator
notes: The article contains the 56-line Cotton witness version, not the complete three-witness record tradition.

related_works.title: King of grace and full of pity / The Fifteen Signs before the Day of Judgment
notes: The article contains the 301-line Cotton witness version. Digby 86 is a collateral witness represented indirectly through Stengel’s comparison text.

evidence.citation: CME00100 XML source in the pinned CME submodule.
evidence.locator: HEADER; TEXT/@LANG; BODY/DIV1 and all DIV2, P, LG, L, NOTE, PB, and MILESTONE descendants
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the complete article boundary, title, languages, editorial framing, Cambridge and Cotton section order, line text, printed pages, historical foliation, note quotations, signature, and addendum.

evidence.citation: Structural and abstract-work boundary audit of CME00100.
evidence.locator: direct DIV2/LG/L and nested NOTE/LG/L sequences
evidence.summary: Counts three P, six LG, 763 total L, 755 direct/base L, and eight nested quotation L. Cambridge is 4 + 394 lines. Cotton is serialized as 74 + 283 direct lines, but DIMEV 436 ends at direct line 56 and DIMEV 3000 begins at direct line 57, yielding 56 + 301 abstract-work lines.
notes: Direct Cotton line 56 ends ‘Thorow Adam synne and Eve also’; direct line 57 begins ‘Kyng of heven, blessed þou be.’

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for Varnhagen’s complete 1880 article.
evidence.summary: Supplies the printed title, article scope, page ranges, editor/transcriber/commentator responsibility, direct Cambridge and Cotton source claims, Stengel comparison, signature, and addendum.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for DIMEV version 1.0 records 5309, 436, and 3000.
evidence.summary: Supplies the three work identities and boundaries, IMEV/NIMEV numbers, names, descriptions, incipits and explicits, couplet form, line counts, witness censuses, loci, linguistic profiles, and edition references.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for the 1857 CUL Ff.2.38 catalog entry.
evidence.summary: Supports the Cambridge witness date, support, broad physical description, historical f. 42v start, manuscript title, and distinction between historical and current foliation.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from the Database of Middle English Romance for CUL Ff.2.38.
evidence.summary: Supports the mid-fifteenth-century witness date, paper support, dimensions, two-scribe description, current target ff. 50v-53r, IMEV 3368, and 1979 paper facsimile.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for the British Library Cotton Caligula A II component record.
evidence.summary: Supports the direct Cotton witness date and origin, broad target loci, adjacent f. 91r-91v contents, and the need to retain the XML/DIMEV locator discrepancy.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for Bodleian MS Digby 86.
evidence.summary: Supports the collateral witness’s 1272-1282 date, Worcestershire manuscript production, material context, and distinction from its target-language localization.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for Stengel’s 1871 Digby 86 edition.
evidence.summary: Supports the indirect comparison path Varnhagen-to-Stengel-to-Digby and prevents modeling Varnhagen as a direct Digby transcriber.

evidence.citation: General Corpus CME00100 lineage manifest and repository XML evidence.
evidence.summary: Supplies stable entity IDs and the layered distinction among repository artifact, U-M encoding, Varnhagen and Stengel editions, physical witnesses, catalogs, and facsimiles.

open_questions.question: When were DIMEV 5309, 436, and 3000 originally composed, as distinct from their surviving witness dates?
next_steps: Review current Fifteen Signs and invocation scholarship for composition ranges or earlier textual analogues.
Add part-scoped composition statements only where the evidence concerns the abstract work rather than one copy.

open_questions.question: Is the Cotton DIMEV 3000 version an adaptation of the Digby-related version, or vice versa?
next_steps: Compare all six DIMEV 3000 witnesses and modern critical scholarship.
Retain Varnhagen’s explicit uncertainty until a reliable stemmatic or version study resolves priority.

open_questions.question: What dialect or text-language localization can be assigned reliably to the Cotton copies of DIMEV 436 and 3000?
next_steps: Consult LALME/eLALME and manuscript-specific linguistic scholarship.
Do not transfer Digby’s north-west Gloucestershire target-language localization or Worcestershire production to Cotton.

open_questions.question: How should the XML/Varnhagen 91v milestone be reconciled with the BL catalog’s 91r ending and DIMEV’s precise 91rb ending?
next_steps: Inspect current color images of ff. 89r-91v and compare foliation, columns, line endings, and adjacent texts.
Do not extend the cataloged Fifteen Signs into the Owayne Miles text beginning at f. 91v.

open_questions.question: Which exact editions or witnesses supplied Varnhagen’s six Cursor Mundi and two Hending quotation lines?
next_steps: Resolve the abbreviated references ‘Curs. Mundi 22493 ff.’ and ‘Lond. text 176’ against Varnhagen’s bibliography and other Anglia 3 sections.
Keep the quotation lines cataloged as editorial evidence rather than base-work content until their source layers are confirmed.

open_questions.question: Should the three contained DIMEV works eventually receive independent descriptive metadata records linked to the article by containment?
next_steps: Create independent work records when the corpus viewer and publication model can associate one encoded article with multiple separately publishable abstract works.
Until then, retain the explicit three-part model and related-work identifiers in CME00100.

notes: The complete article is mixed German prose and Middle English verse; the three medieval component works themselves are verse.
The Cambridge and Cotton direct-source witnesses are distinct from Digby 86, which enters the article through Stengel’s printed comparison text.
Cambridge historical foliation 42v-45r and current foliation 50va-53ra are both retained rather than silently normalizing one away.
Cotton’s broad BL locus ff. 89r-91r and DIMEV’s exact columns ff. 89ra-91rb coexist; the XML 91v conflict remains explicit.
Manuscript holding and edition publication places are not promoted to medieval composition places.
The tags describe content actually encoded in CME00100, not every Fifteen Signs version or comparative work mentioned in Varnhagen’s preface and notes.
